section.title: A Good Soldier of Christ Jesus
section.sectionRationale: Having charged Timothy to guard the deposit, Paul now shows him how to carry and pass it on: be strong in grace, entrust the message to reliable teachers, and endure hardship. Three everyday pictures, the soldier, the athlete, and the farmer, teach single-minded, disciplined, patient endurance. All of it is anchored in the risen Christ and sealed by a trustworthy saying about dying, living, enduring, and God's unbreakable faithfulness.
section.thesis: Strengthened by grace, Timothy is to endure hardship like a focused soldier, a rule-keeping athlete, and a hardworking farmer, remembering the risen Christ for whose gospel Paul suffers in chains, and resting in the trustworthy saying that those who die and endure with Christ will live and reign with Him, for even when we are faithless He remains faithful.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to be strong in grace and entrust the gospel to faithful men; the three images of the soldier, athlete, and farmer that model endurance; and the risen Christ at the center, sealed by the trustworthy saying of verses 11 to 13.

units.label: Be strong and entrust the gospel

verses.text: You therefore, my child, {{endynamoo|be strong}} in the grace that is in Christ Jesus.
And the things that you have heard me say among many witnesses, {{paratithemi|entrust}} these to faithful men who will be qualified to teach others as well.
Join me in suffering, like a good {{stratiotes|soldier}} of Christ Jesus.
A soldier refrains from entangling himself in civilian affairs, in order to please the one who enlisted him.
Likewise, a competitor does not receive the crown unless he competes according to the rules.
The hardworking farmer should be the first to partake of the crops.
Consider what I am saying, for the Lord will give you insight into all things.
Remember Jesus Christ, {{egeiro|raised from the dead}}, descended from David, as proclaimed by my gospel,
for which I suffer to the extent of being chained like a criminal. But the word of God cannot be chained!
For this reason I endure all things for the sake of the elect, so that they too may obtain the salvation that is in Christ Jesus, with eternal glory.
This is a trustworthy saying: If we died with Him, we will also live with Him;
if we endure, we will also reign with Him; if we deny Him, He will also deny us;
if we are faithless, He remains {{pistos|faithful}}, for He cannot deny Himself.

endynamoo.gloss: To be strengthened, to be empowered from within. The form is passive: Timothy is to let himself be made strong, and the strength is located in the grace that is in Christ Jesus, not in his own resolve. Endurance in ministry begins not with gritted teeth but with drawing on the grace God supplies.

paratithemi.gloss: To place beside, to deposit or entrust for safekeeping. It is the verb behind the noun deposit in chapter 1: Timothy commits the apostolic message to trustworthy people as one hands over a treasure. Here it maps a chain of four generations, Paul to Timothy to faithful men to others, the ordinary way the gospel is preserved.

stratiotes.gloss: A soldier. Paul draws on the soldier's single-minded devotion: an enlisted man does not tangle himself in civilian pursuits but aims to please his commanding officer. The Christian worker, likewise, accepts hardship and keeps focus, serving the One who enlisted him rather than being pulled apart by lesser concerns.

egeiro.gloss: To raise up, to wake from the dead. The perfect tense in remember Jesus Christ raised from the dead points to a past event with abiding effect: He was raised and remains the living Lord. This risen Christ, the promised offspring of David, is the sum of Paul's gospel and the ground of all endurance.

pistos.gloss: Faithful, trustworthy, reliable. The word describes the trustworthy saying, the faithful men who receive the deposit, and supremely Christ Himself, who remains faithful even when we are faithless. God's faithfulness is not propped up by ours; it rests on His own unchanging character, for He cannot deny Himself.

basic.context: Paul keeps coaching Timothy for hard ministry. First he tells him to draw strength from God's grace and to pass the gospel on to reliable people who can teach others. Then he uses three familiar pictures, a soldier, an athlete, and a farmer, to show that faithful service means focus, discipline, and hard work. At the center he points to the risen Jesus, and he ends with a memorable saying about staying with Christ through death and hardship.
basic.hermeneutics: The three images are illustrations, not rigid rules, and together they teach one lesson: gospel work costs something and calls for endurance. The trustworthy saying in verses 11 to 13 is likely an early Christian hymn or creed, quoted here to steady believers who are tempted to give up under pressure.
basic.originalLanguages: Timothy is to endynamoo, to be strengthened, in grace, a passive verb that points to strength received, not summoned. He is to paratithemi, to entrust, the message like a deposit. The soldier is a stratiotes, single-minded for his commander. Paul says to remember Christ egeiro, raised from the dead, the risen Lord. And even when we are faithless, God remains pistos, faithful, true to Himself.
basic.typology: Jesus as the offspring of David who is raised from the dead fulfills God's promise of an everlasting king. The soldier, athlete, and farmer picture the believer's whole life of disciplined service that leads to reward.
basic.christ: Everything centers on Jesus Christ, risen from the dead and descended from David, the heart of Paul's gospel. Union with Him is the theme of the closing saying: die with Him and live with Him, endure and reign with Him. He is so faithful that He cannot deny His own nature or His own people.
basic.god: God supplies grace that makes His servants strong, spreads His word so freely that no chain can bind it, and remains faithful even when His people falter. His purpose is the salvation and eternal glory of His elect.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1 also names Jesus as descended from David and declared Son of God by resurrection. First Corinthians 9 pictures the disciplined athlete running to win. Romans 8 promises that if we suffer with Christ we will be glorified with Him.
basic.oneStory: God promised David a son whose kingdom would never end, and that promise lands in the risen Jesus. Now His unstoppable word runs out to gather the elect, carried by servants who endure like soldiers, athletes, and farmers, until they live and reign with the faithful Christ forever.

context.literary: Chapter 2 opens with therefore, drawing consequences from the charge to guard the deposit. Paul stacks three quick occupational images, then grounds them in a compact gospel statement about the risen Christ and his own chains, before sealing the section with a rhythmic, four-line trustworthy saying. The movement runs from command, to illustration, to Christ, to creed.
context.historical: Paul writes from a Roman prison, literally chained like a common criminal, expecting death. Timothy leads in Ephesus, where discouragement and opposition threaten to thin the ranks of faithful teachers. In that setting Paul's concern is succession: the gospel must be entrusted to reliable people so it survives his death.
context.canonical: This is one of several trustworthy sayings in the Pastoral Epistles, likely drawn from early Christian worship. The passage is a classic text on discipleship and endurance and on the security of believers grounded in Christ's unfailing faithfulness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To urge Timothy to draw strength from grace, secure the gospel's future by entrusting it to faithful teachers, and endure hardship with single-minded discipline, all anchored in the risen Christ and the certainty of God's faithfulness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The images of soldier, athlete, and farmer are illustrative, teaching enduring principles of focus, discipline, and diligence rather than prescribing literal military or agricultural practice. The commands to be strong in grace, entrust the gospel, and endure are prescriptive for all who serve Christ.

debates.question: What does if we deny Him, He will also deny us mean in verse 12?

views.label: A solemn warning against final apostasy
views.case: Persistent, settled denial of Christ shows a heart that was never truly His, and such denial meets Christ's own denial, echoing His words in the Gospels.

views.label: Held together with verse 13's assurance
views.case: The next line insists that even our faithlessness cannot make God unfaithful, so the warning targets hardened rejection, not the stumbling of a true but weak believer.

debates.bearing: The saying both warns and comforts: it sobers us against deserting Christ while assuring the weak that God's faithfulness rests on His own unchanging character.

debates.question: Is the trustworthy saying in verses 11 to 13 an early hymn?

views.label: A quoted hymn or creed
views.case: Its balanced, rhythmic lines and the introductory formula this is a trustworthy saying suggest Paul is citing material already sung or confessed in the churches.

views.label: Paul's own compact composition
views.case: Whether borrowed or freshly written, the lines carry apostolic authority and function as a memorable summary of union with Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way, the church is meant to hold these words as a fixed, trustworthy anchor for endurance under pressure.

study.originalLanguages: The section is built on a few weighty words. Timothy is to endynamoo, be strengthened, a passive form: the strength comes to him in the grace that is in Christ, not out of his own will. He is to paratithemi, entrust, the gospel, the verb form of the noun deposit from chapter 1, so that chapter 2 shows how the deposit is passed on: to faithful men who teach others still. The good stratiotes, soldier, will not entangle himself in civilian affairs, picturing undivided devotion. At the center stands Christ egeiro, raised, in the perfect tense, the risen and abidingly living Lord. And the climax rests on pistos, faithful: the trustworthy saying, the faithful men, and above all the Christ who remains faithful when we are not, because He cannot deny Himself.

typology.type: Jesus, the offspring of David, raised from the dead
typology.fulfillment: The promised everlasting king of David's line, whose resurrection secures the kingdom in which His people will reign with Him.
Pictures of the believer's disciplined, enduring service now, crowned with reward and shared glory when Christ appears.

study.christ: Christ is the center of the passage: Jesus Christ, raised from the dead, descended from David, as proclaimed by Paul's gospel. His resurrection is the living heart of the good news for which Paul wears chains, and the promise that the word of God cannot be chained flows from a Lord who is alive. The closing saying draws the believer into union with Him: dying with Him, we live; enduring, we reign; and even our faithlessness cannot overturn His faithfulness, for the risen Christ cannot deny Himself or His own.
study.god: God gives grace that strengthens His servants and pours out His word so powerfully that no imprisonment can restrain it. He works through Paul's endurance for the salvation and eternal glory of His elect. Most strikingly, He remains faithful even when His people are faithless, because faithfulness belongs to His very nature: He cannot deny Himself. His reliability, not ours, is the ground of Christian hope.

crossReferences.note: Jesus descended from David and declared Son of God in power by His resurrection, matching verse 8.
The disciplined athlete competing for the prize, illustrating verse 5.
If we suffer with Him we will also be glorified with Him, echoing die with Him and reign with Him.
Paul's own poured-out endurance and the crown that awaits, showing the pattern he calls Timothy to follow.

study.oneStory: God promised David an heir whose throne would stand forever, and the promise is kept in Jesus, raised from the dead. His unchained word now runs through the world, entrusted from Paul to Timothy to faithful teachers to others still, gathering the elect. Those who endure with the faithful Christ will live and reign with Him, one more stretch of God's long story that ends in shared and eternal glory.

sermon.title: Discipleship in 3D, Part One
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message works through 2 Timothy 2:3-6, the images of the soldier, athlete, and farmer, drawing out the single-minded focus, disciplined obedience, and patient toil that mark faithful endurance in Christ's service.

quiz.q: Where does Timothy find the strength Paul calls him to?

options.text: In the grace that is in Christ Jesus, received rather than self-generated.
options.note: The verb is passive: be strengthened in grace, not by willpower.

options.text: In his own determination and natural courage.
options.note: Paul locates the strength in grace, not in Timothy's resolve.

options.text: In the support of the many faithful teachers around him.
options.note: Faithful teachers matter, but the strength named here is grace in Christ.

options.text: In God's empowering, which he is to draw on for endurance.
options.note: True and close, though the verse specifically names the grace in Christ Jesus.

quiz.q: What is the point of the soldier, athlete, and farmer images?

options.text: Faithful gospel work calls for focus, discipline, and hard, patient labor.
options.note: Together the three picture single-minded, disciplined, diligent endurance.

options.text: Ministry should be easy and free of hardship.
options.note: Each image assumes hardship, rules, and toil, not ease.

options.text: Christians should literally take up soldiering, athletics, or farming.
options.note: These are illustrations of endurance, not literal vocations to adopt.

options.text: Serving Christ requires endurance and self-denial to please Him.
options.note: True in substance, though it slightly narrows the threefold picture of focus, discipline, and toil.

quiz.q: How does the gospel's spread relate to Paul's chains?

options.text: Paul is chained, but the word of God cannot be chained.
options.note: Verse 9 contrasts Paul's bondage with the unstoppable word.

options.text: Paul's imprisonment has silenced the gospel for now.
options.note: The opposite: the word runs free even as Paul is bound.

options.text: The gospel spreads only when preachers are free from suffering.
options.note: Paul endures suffering precisely so the elect may be saved.

options.text: Paul endures all things so the elect may obtain salvation and glory.
options.note: True and stated in verse 10, though the sharp point is that the word itself cannot be chained.

quiz.q: What does Paul most want Timothy to remember at the center of it all?

options.text: Jesus Christ, raised from the dead and descended from David.
options.note: Verse 8 puts the risen Christ, the offspring of David, at the heart.

options.text: The rules that athletes must follow to win.
options.note: That is one illustration, not the center; the center is the risen Christ.

options.text: His own achievements in ministry.
options.note: Paul points away from himself to the risen Jesus.

options.text: The gospel Paul preaches, summed up in the living Lord.
options.note: True, and that gospel is exactly Christ raised from the dead.

quiz.q: What does verse 13 teach about God when we are faithless?

options.text: He remains faithful, because He cannot deny Himself.
options.note: God's faithfulness rests on His own unchanging character.

options.text: He becomes faithless toward us in return.
options.note: The verse says the opposite: He remains faithful.

options.text: His faithfulness depends on how faithful we manage to be.
options.note: Paul roots His faithfulness in Himself, not in our performance.

options.text: His steadfastness holds firm even when ours fails.
options.note: True and comforting, though the ground given is that He cannot deny Himself.

questions: Paul tells Timothy to be strong in grace, not in himself. Where are you trying to endure on willpower instead of drawing on the grace that is in Christ?
The gospel travels from Paul to Timothy to faithful people to others still. Who taught you the faith, and to whom are you entrusting it now?
The soldier keeps free of entangling distractions to please his commander. What lesser concerns are dividing your focus from Christ right now?
Remembering the risen Christ steadied Paul in chains. How would fixing your mind on the living Jesus change the hardship you are facing this week?
When we are faithless, He remains faithful. How does resting your security in God's faithfulness rather than your own change the way you follow Him?

translationNotes.note: Be strong translates a passive verb, be strengthened, pointing to strength received in the grace of Christ rather than summoned from within.
Raised from the dead is in the perfect tense, stressing an accomplished event with abiding result: Christ was raised and remains the living Lord.
The trustworthy saying of verses 11 to 13 is widely thought to be an early Christian hymn or confession; its balanced lines are rendered here as clean prose.
Not part of this movement, but note that some manuscripts read the Lord in place of God, as the BSB footnote observes.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 2:1-13. The poetic line breaks in verses 11 to 13 have been rendered as prose. No em dashes present in this movement's text.
